name: Red Wine

book_claims.claim: Moderate consumption with meals is part of the traditional Mediterranean dietary pattern associated with lower cardiovascular mortality
book_claims.mechanism: Polyphenols (resveratrol, anthocyanins) provide antioxidant effects; consumed only with meals to moderate absorption

supplementary_research.finding: Meta-analysis of 84 studies found a J-shaped relationship between alcohol and cardiovascular mortality. Maximum benefit at 1-2 drinks/day for men, 1 drink/day for women. Wine showed stronger protective association than beer or spirits.

PREDIMED trial analysis found moderate red wine consumption (within Mediterranean diet context) associated with reduced cardiovascular events. Polyphenols -- not ethanol alone -- explained much of the benefit, as evidenced by total urinary polyphenol excretion correlating with reduced mortality.

Global Burden of Disease analysis challenged the J-curve, finding the safest level of alcohol consumption is zero when accounting for all health outcomes (not just cardiovascular). Any cardiovascular benefit is offset by increased cancer risk. Important counterpoint to the book's Mediterranean wine tradition.

culinary_pairings.source: Together accounted for ~25% of calories in traditional Neapolitan diet surveyed by Keys
Traditional accompaniment to bean-based Mediterranean meals
Shared polyphenol profiles; classic flavour affinity
Red wine reduction sauces and braised dishes
Herb-forward red wine marinades and braised meats

seasonality: Available year-round. Quality varies by vintage and region. Wines aged in oak may have higher ellagitannin content. No specific seasonal consumption pattern, but traditionally consumed with meals in Mediterranean cultures.

nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Low oral bioavailability (~1-5%). Rapidly metabolised. Therapeutic doses in studies far exceed what wine provides. The wine benefit likely comes from the total polyphenol matrix, not resveratrol alone.
Responsible for red colour. Antioxidant and anti-inflammatory. Better absorbed in the acidic environment of wine than from most food sources.

nutrient_highlights.amount_per_100g: 25-100 mg per glass (higher in tannic wines)
nutrient_highlights.bioavailability_notes: Cardiovascular protective through endothelial function improvement. Wines from Sardinia and SW France (traditional long-lived populations) tend to be highest.
Raises HDL cholesterol and has anticoagulant effects. But also a Group 1 carcinogen (IARC). Net health effect depends on individual risk profile.

synergies.description: Together accounted for ~25% of calories in traditional Neapolitan diet; combined polyphenol synergy

warnings: The Global Burden of Disease study (2018) found zero alcohol is safest when all health outcomes are considered -- cardiovascular benefit may be offset by cancer risk
Consume ONLY with meals, never independently -- this is how traditional Mediterranean populations consumed wine
Limit to 1 glass for women, 1-2 for men. Benefits disappear and harms increase rapidly beyond moderate intake
